text: Being asked why there was no statue of him in the forum, he replied: I would rather have men ask why there is no statue to me than ask why there is one.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. XIX

text: He trained himself to feel neither shame at poverty nor pride at wealth; and he reckoned that the one thing worth contending for was the honor of living virtuously.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. VI

text: He would not accept from a tyrant what he had a right to claim from the gods: a death that belonged to himself alone.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. LXVI

text: Throughout that night he read the Phaedo twice over, and in the morning, composing himself with an expression of perfect tranquility, he lay down. Those who attended him said they had never seen a man so serene on the edge of death.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. LXX

text: He used to say that his enemies had robbed him of the chance to die with honor, but they had not robbed him of the honor itself—for that lay within him and could not be taken.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. LXV

text: He punished no man in anger, and thought severity only just when it was felt to be no more than the nature of the offence required—and no more.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. IX

text: He was accustomed to walk through the heat and cold without a cloak, not to demonstrate contempt for custom, but to accustom himself to endure whatever circumstances required of him.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. VI

text: When urged to submit and save his life, he answered that he could not consent to owe his safety to one whose rule he regarded as the ruin of the republic—not because he feared death, but because he refused complicity.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. LXIV

text: He thought it better to do right and receive no thanks for it than to do wrong and escape punishment. For the one leaves the soul clear; the other corrupts it—and corruption is a greater loss than any loss of reward.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. XXI

text: He said: I begin to be at peace with myself only when I have reason to be ashamed of something—for then I know that my conscience is still alive.
source: Plutarch, Life of Cato the Younger, Ch. VIII
